chunk_summary: This section addresses discordant HbA1c and fasting glucose results in Indian children, emphasizing anemia correction, OGTT use, and avoiding diagnostic bias in metabolic risk evaluation.
learning_objectives: Understand the impact of iron deficiency anemia and hemoglobinopathies on HbA1c interpretation.
Learn structured pathways for resolving discordant HbA1c and fasting glucose results in children.
Recognize the importance of OGTT in detecting impaired glucose tolerance missed by fasting tests.
Avoid diagnostic anchoring bias in differentiating diabetes types in children.
key_facts: Iron deficiency anemia can falsely elevate HbA1c; iron replacement lowers HbA1c without true glycemic change.
HbA1c of 5.7–6.4% with fasting glucose <100 mg/dL requires anemia assessment and correction before retesting.
Fasting glucose of 100–125 mg/dL with HbA1c <5.7% indicates prediabetes risk; OGTT is recommended in puberty, MASLD, or strong family history.
Discordant glycemic results are common in India due to prevalent hemoglobinopathies and anemia.
Evaluate for pancreatic autoantibodies in cases of diabetes-range glycemia with weight loss, ketosis, or rapid onset.
reference_quote: Iron deficiency is associated with higher HbA1c, and iron replacement can lower HbA1c without true glycemic change. — NGSP
document_key_topics: Discordant HbA1c and fasting glucose interpretation in Indian children
Impact of anemia and hemoglobinopathies on glycemic markers
Structured clinical pathways for dysglycemia and insulin resistance
Avoiding diagnostic bias in pediatric diabetes evaluation
